narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model estimated a 50.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which tipped the scales toward rejection to ensure the university admits students with the strongest predicted odds of bar passage. Your LSAT score of 34 stood out positively, as it's a solid performance that pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, even though it's a bit below the typical applicant's 36.7 average. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the average of about 5.7—had the strongest opposing pull, signaling to the model that your early undergraduate grades placed you in the lower third of your peers, significantly raising the risk of bar failure. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, below the typical 3.21, also worked in your favor to some extent by nudging the outlook toward passing, though not enough to offset other concerns. Meanwhile, your Year 3 decile of 3 (versus an average around 5.5) and upper-middle family income contributed modestly toward the failure prediction, as they suggest a consistent pattern of mid-to-lower academic standing relative to most applicants. Your plan to attend full-time, like the vast majority (over 92%) of students, had a negligible neutral effect. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows stronger early and later decile rankings dragging you down, while your LSAT and GPA provided some counterbalance but couldn't overcome the overall risk profile. This decision reflects how the model weighs these factors holistically against patterns from thousands of past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that key strength, improving your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth since those lower deciles. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas—your upper-middle background gives you solid resources to invest in targeted prep. We're rooting for your next steps; persistence pays off in law.
